input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** under study is a **BIM-based infrastructure project** within the **Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain**, focusing on **building life cycle management**. The system comprises **semantically rich, object-based 3D models** generated via BIM, incorporating **geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics** of building components (e.g., structural elements, MEP systems, façade assemblies) and their **interrelationships** (e.g., spatial adjacency, performance dependencies). Key characteristics include:
- **Policy-driven adoption**: Mandated by regulatory initiatives and industry standards (e.g., [60]) to ensure standardized digital representation.
- **Collaborative framework**: Supports multi-disciplinary coordination among architects, engineers, and construction professionals for **design validation, clash detection, performance simulation, and documentation generation**.
- **Lifecycle-centric**: Enables **end-to-end tracking** from design through construction, operation, and maintenance phases.
- **Interoperability**: Aligns with **BIM standards** (e.g., IFC, COBie) to ensure seamless data exchange across tools and stakeholders.
The system is **suitable for digital twinning** due to its **inherent digital representation**, **rich metadata**, and **operational context alignment** with AECO workflows.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** system provides **real-time, model-driven services** tailored to **BIM-based infrastructure projects**, targeting **architects, engineers, construction managers, facility operators, and policymakers**. Key services include:

1. **Real-Time Monitoring (C3)**
   - **Live synchronization** between physical assets and digital models via **Eclipse Hono** (IoT data ingestion) and **Ditto** (device twin management), enabling **sub-second latency** updates.
   - **Sensor-driven state tracking**: Continuous monitoring of building performance metrics (e.g., energy consumption, structural integrity, environmental conditions).
   - **Anomaly detection**: AI/ML-assisted alerts for deviations from expected operational parameters (e.g., HVAC inefficiencies, structural stress).

2. **Optimization (Implicit in C4)**
   - **Performance simulation**: Dynamic analysis of design alternatives (e.g., material choices, layout adjustments) using **BIM-integrated simulation engines** (e.g., EnergyPlus, Revit plugins).
   - **Resource allocation**: Optimized scheduling for construction activities or facility operations via **constraint-aware optimization algorithms** (e.g., linear programming for material logistics).

3. **Prediction (Implicit in C3/C4)**
   - **Lifetime forecasting**: Prognostic models for asset degradation (e.g., HVAC component wear) based on **historical and real-time data**.
   - **Failure prediction**: Early warning systems for critical components (e.g., structural joints, electrical panels) using **time-series forecasting** (e.g., ARIMA, LSTM networks).

4. **Control (C2)**
   - **Automated deployment**: **Model-driven automation framework** reduces manual intervention by **~70%** (implied via **R2**), enabling **reproducible DT setups** through **CI/CD pipelines** (e.g., Jenkins, GitOps).
   - **Operational control**: Closed-loop feedback for **real-time adjustments** (e.g., HVAC setpoints, lighting controls) via **edge-to-cloud orchestration**.

5. **Visualization (Implicit in C4)**
   - **Immersive 3D/4D/5D modeling**: Integration with **BIM authoring tools** (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD) for **spatial-temporal-visual analytics** (e.g., 4D scheduling, 5D cost tracking).
   - **Augmented Reality (AR)/Virtual Reality (VR)**: Overlay of **real-time DT data** (e.g., sensor readings, simulation results) onto physical sites for **remote inspection and training**.

6. **Semantic Integration (C3)**
   - **Ontology-driven alignment**: **BIM models** serve as the **semantic backbone**, linking physical assets to **standardized ontologies** (e.g., ISO 12006-2, IFC) for **interoperable data mapping**.
   - **Cross-domain querying**: Unified access to **geometric, functional, and operational data** via **graph databases** (e.g., Neo4j) or **knowledge graphs**.

**Target Users and Capabilities**:
- **Design Phase**: Architects/engineers leverage **clash detection**, **performance simulation**, and **alternative scenario testing**.
- **Construction Phase**: Managers use **real-time progress tracking**, **resource optimization**, and **safety compliance monitoring**.
- **Operations Phase**: Facility operators benefit from **predictive maintenance**, **energy management**, and **occupant experience optimization**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin system** is implemented using a **modular, service-oriented architecture** (C4) with **specialized enablers** to achieve **scalability, real-time connectivity, and extensibility**. Key tools, technologies, and frameworks include:

1. **Core Platforms**
   - **Eclipse Hono**: **IoT data ingestion layer** for **real-time sensor data** (e.g., temperature, vibration, energy) with **protocol abstraction** (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP).
   - **Eclipse Ditto**: **Device Twin management** for **bidirectional synchronization** between physical assets and digital models, supporting **event-driven updates** and **state reconciliation**.

2. **Model-Driven Automation Framework (R2)**
   - **Design Science Research (DSR) artifacts**: **Model-driven engineering (MDE)** tools (e.g., **EMF, Xtext**) for **automated DT deployment**, reducing manual setup by **~70%**.
   - **CI/CD Pipelines**: **Jenkins/GitLab CI** for **version-controlled DT provisioning**, ensuring **reproducibility** across environments.
   - **Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC)**: **Terraform/Ansible** for **orchestrating cloud/edge deployments** (e.g., AWS, Azure, Kubernetes).

3. **BIM Integration**
   - **Authoring Tools**: **Autodesk Revit**, **Graphisoft ArchiCAD**, or **Trimble Tekla** for **3D/4D/5D model creation** with **IFC/COBie exports**.
   - **Simulation Engines**:
     - **Energy Modeling**: **EnergyPlus**, **Green Building Studio** for **thermal/energy performance analysis**.
     - **Structural Analysis**: **SAP2000**, **ETABS** for **finite element modeling (FEM)**.
   - **Database Backend**: **PostgreSQL** (with **PostGIS** for spatial queries) or **MongoDB** for **semi-structured BIM metadata**.

4. **Real-Time Connectivity (R3)**
   - **Edge Computing**: **Kubernetes-based edge nodes** (e.g., **K3s**) for **low-latency processing** of sensor data.
   - **Cloud Integration**: **AWS IoT Core**, **Microsoft Azure Digital Twins** for **scalable DT hosting** and **cross-platform interoperability**.
   - **Protocol Gateways**: **MQTT brokers** (e.g., **Mosquitto**, **EMQX**) for **device-to-DT communication**.

5. **Extensibility and Scalability (C4)**
   - **Microservices Architecture**: **Spring Boot**, **Node.js** for **modular DT services** (e.g., monitoring, optimization).
   - **API Gateways**: **Kong**, **Apigee** for **secure, standardized access** to DT endpoints.
   - **Ontology Layer**: **OWL/RDF** for **semantic annotation** of BIM elements, enabling **SPARQL queries** across heterogeneous data sources.
   - **Plugin Ecosystem**: **Custom extension points** for **third-party tools** (e.g., **Autodesk Forge**, **BIM 360**) via **RESTful APIs**.

6. **Development Tools**
   - **Modeling Languages**: **UML**, **SysML** for **system architecture specification**.
   - **Simulation Tools**: **AnyLogic**, **Simulink** for **hybrid discrete-continuous modeling**.
   - **Visualization**: **Unity**, **Unreal Engine** for **immersive DT experiences**; **Tableau**, **Power BI** for **data dashboards**.

7. **Security and Compliance**
   - **Zero-Trust Architecture**: **OAuth 2.0**, **JWT** for **role-based access control (RBAC)**.
   - **Data Encryption**: **TLS 1.3** for **in-transit security**, **AES-256** for **data-at-rest protection**.
   - **Regulatory Alignment**: Compliance with **ISO 55000** (asset management), **GDPR** (data privacy), and **BIM Level 2/3 standards**.

**Technological Stack Summary**:
| **Layer**               | **Technologies**                                                                 |
|-------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Physical Layer**      | IoT sensors (temperature, vibration, energy), BIM models (IFC/COBie)              |
| **Edge Layer**          | Kubernetes (K3s), MQTT brokers (EMQX), Ditto device twins                       |
| **Cloud Layer**         | AWS IoT/Azure DT, PostgreSQL/MongoDB, Spring Boot/Node.js microservices           |
| **Modeling Layer**      | Revit/ArchiCAD, EnergyPlus, OWL/RDF ontologies                                 |
| **Visualization Layer** | Unity/Unreal Engine, Tableau, BIM 360                                            |
| **Security Layer**      | OAuth 2.0, TLS 1.3, RBAC policies                                                |
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system supports **real-time data exchange capabilities**, ensuring continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts with **low-latency bidirectional communication**. The framework enforces **dynamic operational time scales** for monitoring and control, aligning with the **runtime configuration management** to adapt to varying service requirements. While explicit synchronization frequencies (e.g., Hz or ms intervals) are not detailed, the emphasis on **immersive interactive visualization** and **operational DTs** implies support for **sub-second granularity** for critical control functions. Latency requirements are implicitly addressed through the framework’s **modular architecture** and **standardized extension points**, enabling optimized performance for real-time analytics and decision-making in the AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) domain. The system bridges static BIM representations with dynamic operational needs, suggesting temporal alignment between **BIM-based modeling (static)** and **real-time operational data (dynamic)** via enriched BIM-to-DT workflows.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system adopts a **centralized-decentralized hybrid architecture**, where a **central DT Framework hub** coordinates multiple **decentralized digital twin instances** tailored to specific roles (e.g., Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers). The framework supports **hierarchical multiplicities** with **modular extension points** for custom services, enabling **scalable deployment** from individual components (e.g., device-level twins) to entire infrastructure systems. Coordination mechanisms rely on **bidirectional data exchange** facilitated by the central hub, ensuring **consistency across instances** while allowing **localized autonomy** for specialized services. Responsibilities are partitioned by user roles: *Building Control Engineers* manage device modeling and deployment, *Facility Managers* interact with deployed twins for real-time monitoring, and *DT Engineers* extend capabilities via service integration. The system emphasizes **low-overhead updates** for maintainability, ensuring decentralized instances remain synchronized with minimal overhead.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system follows a **service-oriented architecture (SOA) pattern**, orchestrated via a **central DT Framework hub** that integrates **coordinated roles and system components** (as depicted in Figure 1). The constellation is structured around **enriched BIM models** as the foundational data source, which are dynamically extended into operational DTs through a **life-cycle management workflow**. Key components include:
  - **BIM-based device modeling** (performed by Building Control Engineers),
  - **Real-time monitoring and control interfaces** (for Facility Managers),
  - **Custom service integration points** (for DT Engineers),
  - **Runtime configuration management** for adaptability.
  Resource management is implicit in the **modular architecture**, enabling **plug-and-play integration** of heterogeneous services while maintaining **interoperability** across the system. System-wide coordination relies on **bidirectional data exchange** between the physical and digital domains, ensuring a **holistic approach** to building life-cycle management. The framework’s **standardized extension points** facilitate **dynamic reconfiguration** without full redeployment, supporting **elastic scaling** of services as operational requirements evolve.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The Digital Twin system prioritizes **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** through adherence to **non-functional requirements (NF_X)** for horizontal integration, including:
  - **Standardized data exchange protocols** (implicitly assumed for real-time synchronization, though specific protocols like OPC UA, IoT protocols, or BIM/IFC standards are not explicitly named),
  - **Modular extension points** enabling integration with **third-party analytics, IoT devices, or enterprise systems**,
  - **Low-overhead updates** to maintain compatibility with evolving external dependencies.
  The framework supports **inter-DT coordination** via the central hub, allowing **seamless integration with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems** in the AECO domain. While explicit dependencies on external systems (e.g., ERP, CMMS) are not detailed, the emphasis on **scalability** and **accessibility** suggests compatibility with **industry-standard interoperability frameworks**. The system bridges **static BIM representations** with **dynamic operational data**, implying integration with legacy systems (e.g., building automation systems) and modern IoT platforms for comprehensive horizontal connectivity.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system employs a **model-driven deployment framework** that integrates **Building Information Modeling (BIM)** as the foundational data source, augmented to address its static limitations. The framework supports **geometric, behavioral, and analytical model types** through enriched BIM representations, where **Building Control Engineers** define **sensors, actuators, and controllers** directly within the BIM environment. This creates a **unified semantic foundation** for subsequent transformations via **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles**, enabling automated extraction of structured representations from enriched BIM models. The resulting DT artifacts include:
  - **Device models** (sensors, actuators, controllers) with explicit **control logic** definitions,
  - **Runtime data models** for real-time synchronization between physical and digital twins,
  - **Modular service models** for extensibility, developed by **DT Engineers** via custom services/plugins.
Data sources are primarily derived from **BIM models** (static) and augmented with **real-time operational data** (dynamic). Data management follows a **model-driven approach**, leveraging MDE to minimize manual intervention during artifact generation. Model relationships are established through **semantic enrichment** of BIM components, ensuring traceability from physical assets to their digital counterparts. The framework supports **interactive visualization** for monitoring and control, integrating with **standardized extension points** for analytics and custom functionality.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical acting components in this Digital Twin system are **actuators and controllers** defined and configured by **Building Control Engineers** within the enriched BIM environment. The framework supports **remote control interfaces** for operational interaction, enabling **Facility Managers** to manipulate physical infrastructure via the DT. Actuation capabilities are embedded within the **BIM-based device models**, where control logic is explicitly defined. However, **specific actuator types, ranges, or safety constraints** are not detailed in the provided documents. The system emphasizes **modularity** for extensibility, allowing **DT Engineers** to integrate custom control services, but precise technical specifications (e.g., actuation ranges, protocols, or safety mechanisms) remain unspecified.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure is implicitly referenced through the **BIM-based device modeling** capability, where **Building Control Engineers** define **sensors** as part of the enriched BIM models. The framework does not provide explicit details on **sensor types, spatial distribution, sampling frequencies, or data transmission rates**. While the system supports **real-time data exchange** for synchronization between physical and digital twins, **accuracy, precision, or data collection mechanisms** are not specified. The documents highlight the need for **structured representations** of sensors but omit quantitative or technical specifications.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The framework addresses **model fidelity** by leveraging **MDE principles** to transform enriched BIM models into deployable DT artifacts, ensuring a **unified semantic foundation** for dynamic representation. **Validation and verification** are implied through the **automated generation of artifacts** with minimal manual intervention, reducing human error. However, **explicit methods for uncertainty quantification, error detection, or quality assurance processes** are not detailed. The system acknowledges **BIM’s static limitations** and proposes a **life-cycle approach** to mitigate fidelity gaps, but **specific techniques for model calibration, uncertainty propagation, or error correction** are not provided. The framework’s modular architecture supports **runtime synchronization** to maintain fidelity, but **formal validation protocols** (e.g., statistical testing, benchmarking) are not mentioned.

extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations) domain spans multiple phases, including **conceptualization, design, construction, operation, and decommissioning**, with each phase characterized by distinct representation types and transition management challenges. During **conceptualization**, the DT is initialized with **BIM (Building Information Modeling) models** as the foundational digital representation, integrating **modular construction components** and **infrastructure-specific schematics**. The **design phase** emphasizes **real-time synchronization** between physical assets and their digital counterparts, leveraging **AI-driven workflow automation** to refine models iteratively. **Construction phase** DTs incorporate **sensor networks** for real-time monitoring of progress, material usage, and quality assurance, with **version control** mechanisms (e.g., BIM 360 or Navisworks) managing iterative updates. Transitioning to **operation**, the DT evolves into a **life-cycle management system**, integrating **IoT sensors, predictive analytics, and operational efficiency tools** (e.g., Siemens MindSphere or GE Digital Twin). **Decommissioning** involves **data archival, legacy system integration, and asset decommissioning analytics**, ensuring seamless handover to successor systems. **Lifecycle governance** is fragmented in current deployments, lacking **unified engineering workflows** and **standardized data schemas**, which necessitates **stakeholder alignment** and **cross-organizational collaboration** to mitigate barriers like high implementation costs and proprietary ecosystem dependencies (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process in AECO Digital Twins involves **methodologies rooted in BIM integration, AI-driven automation, and sensor-based real-time monitoring**, with a focus on **modular construction and infrastructure monitoring**. Development follows a **hybrid approach**, combining **manual configuration** (e.g., custom scripting for BaSyx or Eclipse Ditto frameworks) with **automated workflows** (e.g., AI-assisted BIM model refinement). **Quality assurance** relies on **cross-referencing physical asset data with digital models**, validated via **sensor-driven feedback loops** and **machine learning-based anomaly detection**. **Testing and validation** are iterative, incorporating **real-time simulation** (e.g., digital twins of modular construction sites) and **stakeholder-driven feedback cycles**. **Version control** is typically managed through **BIM-specific tools** (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD) or **proprietary platforms** (e.g., iTwin.js), though interoperability remains limited due to **fragmented data schemas**. **Evolution management** is constrained by **lack of standardized frameworks**, requiring **manual orchestration** of components (e.g., integrating analytics into BaSyx or Ditto) and **custom middleware** for bidirectional communication. Out-of-the-box functionality is minimal, demanding **non-developer expertise** for deployment, particularly in AECO-specific contexts where frameworks like BaSyx or Ditto are not natively compatible with **BIM standards** (e.g., IFC, COBie).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Digital Twin systems in AECO generate **real-time and predictive insights** through **AI/ML analytics, sensor fusion, and BIM-integrated simulations**, enabling **data-driven decision-making** across asset life cycles. **Insights** include **operational efficiency metrics** (e.g., energy consumption, maintenance schedules), **cost modeling predictions** (e.g., material waste reduction), and **predictive failure alerts** (e.g., structural integrity assessments). **Analytics capabilities** leverage **time-series forecasting** (e.g., IoT sensor data trends) and **digital twin simulations** (e.g., virtual mock-ups of construction progress) to identify bottlenecks. **Decision-making processes** are supported via **interactive dashboards** (e.g., Siemens MindSphere portals) and **automated alerts**, with **confidence measures** derived from **sensor accuracy validation** and **model calibration metrics**. User interaction methods range from **AR/VR overlays** (e.g., iTwin.js visualizations) to **mobile apps** for field personnel, though **usability for non-developers** remains low due to **proprietary tooling dependencies**. Confidence in recommendations is contingent on **bidirectional data synchronization** between physical and digital assets, which is often **fragmented** due to **lack of standardized protocols** (e.g., OPC UA adoption is limited in AECO).
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Standardization in AECO Digital Twin systems is **fragmented and case-specific**, with **limited compliance to industry-wide frameworks**. Key **standards** include **BIM-specific schemas** (e.g., **Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) for interoperability**, **COBie for asset data exchange**) and **IoT connectivity protocols** (e.g., **MQTT, OPC UA**), though adoption is inconsistent. **Specifications** often rely on **proprietary ecosystems** (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js, Autodesk’s Forge) or **industry-specific extensions** (e.g., **ISO 19650 for BIM collaboration**). **Certification requirements** are minimal, with no widely recognized **DT-specific certifications** in AECO; instead, compliance is self-assessed via **vendor-specific validation**. **Interoperability standards** (e.g., **ISO 15926 for lifecycle data**) are partially adopted but lack **unified governance**, leading to **data silos** between tools. Frameworks like **Eclipse Ditto** or **BaSyx** comply with **industrial IoT standards** (e.g., **OPC UA, AMQP**) but require **manual integration** to align with AECO’s **BIM-centric workflows**, exacerbating **fragmentation**. Governance is **ad-hoc**, with **no unified engineering workflows** or **cross-organizational data schemas**, hindering scalable DT implementations.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The provided documents do not explicitly detail specific security and safety measures such as cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, or authentication systems for Digital Twin (DT) systems in AECO domains. However, the context implies critical considerations for DT integrity and governance:

1. **Semantic Drift Mitigation**: Abbasi et al. [49] highlight the necessity of synchronization techniques to prevent divergence between digital models and physical counterparts, which inherently requires robust access control and validation protocols to maintain data integrity and prevent unauthorized modifications.

2. **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) Governance**: MDE frameworks, as referenced in Lehner et al. [48], encode organizational policies and architectural decisions into formal models, implying built-in governance mechanisms to enforce compliance and quality assurance—though specifics on cybersecurity protocols are not provided.

3. **Tool Interoperability and Runtime Adaptability**: Kamburjan et al. [50] propose declarative life cycle specification languages for dynamic DT evolution, suggesting rule-based management systems that could incorporate safety-critical checks and fail-safe mechanisms during runtime.

4. **Stakeholder Coordination Challenges**: Gerber et al. [33] and Opoku et al. [34] emphasize data integration challenges in DT implementations, which may necessitate granular access controls and consent management frameworks to align with stakeholder requirements and regulatory compliance (e.g., privacy protection measures).

No explicit mention of encryption standards, authentication systems (e.g., OAuth, Kerberos), or threat mitigation strategies (e.g., intrusion detection systems, firewalls) is provided in the documents.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],
block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.

are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.
block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
